FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data explicitly: 'from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study from June 1, 2016, to October 15, 2018' with 3419 adolescents at baseline ages 9-11.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/screen use variable: 'Screen use was assessed using the youth-reported Youth Screen Time Survey as total screen time spent on typical days. A total daily screen use was calculated as a weighted mean across weekdays and weekends.'

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports inferential statistical tests linking screen use to sleep outcomes via mediation analysis. Pathway analyses with regression coefficients: 'The indirect association of parental monitoring with better sleep quality was mediated through decreased screen use (β = 0.004; 95% CI, 0.002-0.008; proportion mediated [PM], 7.3%).' Multiple mediation models test screen use as mediator linking parental factors to sleep outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric values reported: β coefficients, 95% confidence intervals, p-values throughout Results. Examples: 'screen use partially mediated associations, β = 0.004; 95% CI, 0.002-0.008'; 'family conflict to later chronotype, β = 0.01; 95% CI, 0.01-0.08; PM, 25.5%'; parental psychopathology effects β = −0.003; 95% CI, −0.01 to −0.001; PM, 1.0%.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective cohort study with 3 waves (ages 9-11, 12-13, 13-14), linear mixed models, mediation analyses using path analysis, moderation analyses with interaction terms, and sensitivity analyses with multiple imputation.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in JAMA Network Open 2025;8(9):e2531333.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Although parental factors are demographic/contextual predictors of screen use, screen use is NOT the dependent variable in every analysis—it serves as a MEDIATOR between parental factors and sleep outcomes. The outcome variables are sleep dimensions (duration, timing, chronotype, quality, regularity). Additionally, the paper tests mediation pathways involving individual psychological variables (emotional regulation) and cross-sectional associations. Under FT-EC7 rules, the criterion requires 'smartphone/SM is the DEPENDENT variable in every reported analysis involving smartphone/SM' which is not satisfied here.
notes: Paper analyzes prospective associations between parental factors at wave 1 and sleep outcomes at wave 3, with screen use and emotional regulation as partial mediators measured at wave 2. Screen use is consistently a mediating variable, not an outcome. Six mediation models reported (Figure 2: A–G), all examining screen use's role in linking parental factors to sleep. Includes sex moderation analysis (parental warmth × sex interaction on chronotype in girls). INCLUDE paper in review.
